ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper proposes a distinctive representation (TextFlow) that models text pairs as continuous curves and explicitly borrows DNA sequence alignment ideas to combine word positions with sequence matching, which is a novel perspective compared to the listed baselines. However, the core components (sequence alignment concepts, position-aware matching, and a simple parameterized combination with an optional identity-activation XF layer) appear as moderate adaptations of existing techniques rather than a substantially new theoretical framework. Given the use of standard NLP datasets and classical baselines, the contribution reads as an incremental but moderately novel method rather than a highly original breakthrough. | B(3): The paper proposes a distinct variant (TextFlow) that casts text-pair similarity as continuous curves inspired by DNA alignment and combines word-position information with sequence matching, which is a reasonably new perspective. However, core elements (sequence alignment, LCS/Levenshtein-style matching, simple parametric weighting) are well-established, and the optional XF neural layer with identity activation offers limited technical novelty. The authors do clearly differentiate their approach from many classical baselines and cover related work thoroughly, so the contribution reads as a moderate, incremental novelty rather than a highly original breakthrough.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a distinctive representation (TextFlow) that models text pairs as continuous curves and explicitly borrows DNA sequence alignment ideas to combine word positions with sequence matching, which is a novel perspective compared to the listed baselines. However, the core components (sequence alignment concepts, position-aware matching, and a simple parameterized combination with an optional identity-activation XF layer) appear as moderate adaptations of existing techniques rather than a substantially new theoretical framework. Given the use of standard NLP datasets and classical baselines, the contribution reads as an incremental but moderately novel method rather than a highly original breakthrough.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a distinct variant (TextFlow) that casts text-pair similarity as continuous curves inspired by DNA alignment and combines word-position information with sequence matching, which is a reasonably new perspective. However, core elements (sequence alignment, LCS/Levenshtein-style matching, simple parametric weighting) are well-established, and the optional XF neural layer with identity activation offers limited technical novelty. The authors do clearly differentiate their approach from many classical baselines and cover related work thoroughly, so the contribution reads as a moderate, incremental novelty rather than a highly original breakthrough.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(5): The paper addresses text similarity — a core NLP topic — and evaluates on standard, high-profile datasets (SNLI, RTE series, SemEval) and tasks (paraphrase detection, entailment, relevance ranking) that are central to top NLP/ML venues. The proposed method combines sequence-alignment-inspired modeling with a tunable/learnable XF layer and is compared against a comprehensive set of classical baselines, matching the methodological and evaluation expectations for such conferences. Overall the scope, audience, task formulation, and evaluation choices align well with a top-tier NLP/ML venue. | B(4): The paper addresses text similarity — a core NLP topic — and evaluates on standard benchmarks (SNLI, RTE 1–3, SemEval) and relevant tasks (paraphrase detection, entailment, ranking), so it targets the right audience. Its formulation and use of datasets/metrics align with expectations for a top NLP/ML venue. A minor concern is that the reported baselines are largely classical string/overlap measures (Cosine, Jaccard, LCS, Levenshtein, etc.) and there is only an optional neural XF layer, so reviewers may expect stronger contemporary neural baselines for full top-tier suitability.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses text similarity — a core NLP topic — and evaluates on standard, high-profile datasets (SNLI, RTE series, SemEval) and tasks (paraphrase detection, entailment, relevance ranking) that are central to top NLP/ML venues. The proposed method combines sequence-alignment-inspired modeling with a tunable/learnable XF layer and is compared against a comprehensive set of classical baselines, matching the methodological and evaluation expectations for such conferences. Overall the scope, audience, task formulation, and evaluation choices align well with a top-tier NLP/ML venue.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses text similarity — a core NLP topic — and evaluates on standard benchmarks (SNLI, RTE 1–3, SemEval) and relevant tasks (paraphrase detection, entailment, ranking), so it targets the right audience. Its formulation and use of datasets/metrics align with expectations for a top NLP/ML venue. A minor concern is that the reported baselines are largely classical string/overlap measures (Cosine, Jaccard, LCS, Levenshtein, etc.) and there is only an optional neural XF layer, so reviewers may expect stronger contemporary neural baselines for full top-tier suitability.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper tackles the important problem of measuring text similarity and introduces a novel representation (TextFlow) that models text pairs as continuous curves inspired by DNA sequence alignment and combines word positions with sequence matching. It reports encouraging results across eight datasets (including SNLI, RTE 1–3, SemEval-16-3B, SemEval-14-1) and includes tunable parameters (α, β, γ) with an optional XF layer for task-specific training. However, the evaluation appears to compare mainly against classical string- and token-based baselines (Cosine, Jaccard, Levenshtein, LCS, etc.) and code availability is not mentioned, which limits immediate adoption and reduces the likelihood of shifting current researcher/practitioner practice. Overall, the contribution is interesting and could open follow-up work but is unlikely to be transformative without stronger comparisons and broader adoption resources. | B(3): The paper tackles the important problem of text similarity by proposing TextFlow, a novel measure inspired by DNA alignment that represents text pairs as continuous curves and combines word positions with sequence matching. It demonstrates encouraging results across eight datasets (including SNLI, RTE 1–3, and SemEval tasks) for paraphrase detection, entailment, and relevance ranking, indicating practical utility. However, comparisons are mainly against classical baselines (e.g., cosine, Jaccard, Levenshtein, LCS), code availability is not mentioned, and no limitations are stated, so while the representation could open new follow-up work it is unlikely to immediately transform how the field thinks about text similarity.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles the important problem of measuring text similarity and introduces a novel representation (TextFlow) that models text pairs as continuous curves inspired by DNA sequence alignment and combines word positions with sequence matching. It reports encouraging results across eight datasets (including SNLI, RTE 1–3, SemEval-16-3B, SemEval-14-1) and includes tunable parameters (α, β, γ) with an optional XF layer for task-specific training. However, the evaluation appears to compare mainly against classical string- and token-based baselines (Cosine, Jaccard, Levenshtein, LCS, etc.) and code availability is not mentioned, which limits immediate adoption and reduces the likelihood of shifting current researcher/practitioner practice. Overall, the contribution is interesting and could open follow-up work but is unlikely to be transformative without stronger comparisons and broader adoption resources.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles the important problem of text similarity by proposing TextFlow, a novel measure inspired by DNA alignment that represents text pairs as continuous curves and combines word positions with sequence matching. It demonstrates encouraging results across eight datasets (including SNLI, RTE 1–3, and SemEval tasks) for paraphrase detection, entailment, and relevance ranking, indicating practical utility. However, comparisons are mainly against classical baselines (e.g., cosine, Jaccard, Levenshtein, LCS), code availability is not mentioned, and no limitations are stated, so while the representation could open new follow-up work it is unlikely to immediately transform how the field thinks about text similarity.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper’s main claim (TextFlow as a novel, DNA-alignment-inspired similarity measure) is clearly stated and appears to have empirical support, but the summary omits key details needed to fully validate those claims: mathematical derivations/proofs are not mentioned and the justification for representing texts as continuous curves is not documented. The experimental methodology raises concerns because the claimed “eight datasets” is inconsistent with the six datasets listed (SNLI, RTE1–3, SemEval), evaluation metrics are opaque (listed as “CORE”), and baselines are largely simple lexical/string measures rather than modern embedding- or learned baselines. Given these gaps and some unsupported assumptions (e.g., the role and necessity of the XF layer/parameters), the technical evidence is incomplete though not evidently invalid. | B(3): The paper clearly states its main claim (TextFlow based on continuous sequences) and reports encouraging experiments on multiple datasets (SNLI, RTE1-3, SemEval subsets), but the summary lacks evidence of mathematical proofs or completeness of derivations for the proposed measure. Experimental methodology is only partially convincing: a set of classical baselines is compared, hyperparameters are reported, but the evaluation metric ("CORE") and absence of mentioned statistical tests or modern baselines and missing code reduce reproducibility and rigour. Overall the contribution is plausible and partially supported by experiments, but there are notable gaps in theoretical justification and experimental controls.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main claim (TextFlow as a novel, DNA-alignment-inspired similarity measure) is clearly stated and appears to have empirical support, but the summary omits key details needed to fully validate those claims: mathematical derivations/proofs are not mentioned and the justification for representing texts as continuous curves is not documented. The experimental methodology raises concerns because the claimed “eight datasets” is inconsistent with the six datasets listed (SNLI, RTE1–3, SemEval), evaluation metrics are opaque (listed as “CORE”), and baselines are largely simple lexical/string measures rather than modern embedding- or learned baselines. Given these gaps and some unsupported assumptions (e.g., the role and necessity of the XF layer/parameters), the technical evidence is incomplete though not evidently invalid.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper clearly states its main claim (TextFlow based on continuous sequences) and reports encouraging experiments on multiple datasets (SNLI, RTE1-3, SemEval subsets), but the summary lacks evidence of mathematical proofs or completeness of derivations for the proposed measure. Experimental methodology is only partially convincing: a set of classical baselines is compared, hyperparameters are reported, but the evaluation metric ("CORE") and absence of mentioned statistical tests or modern baselines and missing code reduce reproducibility and rigour. Overall the contribution is plausible and partially supported by experiments, but there are notable gaps in theoretical justification and experimental controls.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper evaluates TextFlow on multiple standard datasets (SNLI, RTE 1–3, SemEval tasks) which supports the claim of broad testing, but the reported baselines are largely classical string/similarity measures (Cosine, Jaccard, LCS, Levenshtein, etc.) with no mention of strong modern neural or state-of-the-art baselines, leaving a gap in experimental thoroughness. Ablation or sensitivity analyses are not clearly reported despite the presence of tunable parameters (α, β, γ) and an optional XF layer, and no failure cases or limitations are discussed. Reproducibility is partial: hyperparameters are reported and defaults given, but code availability is not mentioned and the evaluation metric is ambiguously listed as “CORE,” limiting exact replication. | B(3): The paper evaluates TextFlow on multiple standard datasets across paraphrase detection, entailment, and ranking (SNLI, RTE sets, SemEval), which gives reasonable task coverage, and reports hyperparameters and related work coverage as thorough. However, the experimental baselines are primarily lexical/string measures (Cosine, Jaccard, LEV, LCS, etc.) with no mention of contemporary neural baselines, and the summary does not indicate ablation/sensitivity analyses were performed. Failure cases or limitations are not discussed, and while hyperparameters are reported the absence of code and an unclear evaluation metric (“CORE”) reduce reproducibility. Overall the empirical work is adequate but missing important analyses and stronger baselines to fully support the claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates TextFlow on multiple standard datasets (SNLI, RTE 1–3, SemEval tasks) which supports the claim of broad testing, but the reported baselines are largely classical string/similarity measures (Cosine, Jaccard, LCS, Levenshtein, etc.) with no mention of strong modern neural or state-of-the-art baselines, leaving a gap in experimental thoroughness. Ablation or sensitivity analyses are not clearly reported despite the presence of tunable parameters (α, β, γ) and an optional XF layer, and no failure cases or limitations are discussed. Reproducibility is partial: hyperparameters are reported and defaults given, but code availability is not mentioned and the evaluation metric is ambiguously listed as “CORE,” limiting exact replication.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates TextFlow on multiple standard datasets across paraphrase detection, entailment, and ranking (SNLI, RTE sets, SemEval), which gives reasonable task coverage, and reports hyperparameters and related work coverage as thorough. However, the experimental baselines are primarily lexical/string measures (Cosine, Jaccard, LEV, LCS, etc.) with no mention of contemporary neural baselines, and the summary does not indicate ablation/sensitivity analyses were performed. Failure cases or limitations are not discussed, and while hyperparameters are reported the absence of code and an unclear evaluation metric (“CORE”) reduce reproducibility. Overall the empirical work is adequate but missing important analyses and stronger baselines to fully support the claims.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper evaluates TextFlow against a broad set of classical string and set-based baselines (Cosine, Euclidean, Jaccard, Levenshtein, LCS, etc.) on standard datasets (SNLI, RTE 1–3, SemEval) with reported hyperparameters, suggesting largely fair experimental conditions. However, the summary does not indicate comparison to recent neural sentence-embedding or transformer-based baselines (e.g., SBERT/BERT-style methods) and does not report acknowledging when prior methods perform comparably or better. While related work coverage is described as thorough, the empirical evaluation omits key modern baselines, so the comparisons are incomplete. | B(3): The paper evaluates TextFlow against a wide set of classical string- and set-based baselines (Cosine, Euclidean, Word Overlap, Dice, Jaccard, Damerau, Jaro-Winkler, Levenshtein, LCS) on multiple datasets (SNLI, RTE 1–3, SemEval subsets) and reports hyperparameters, indicating generally fair experimental conditions. However, it omits modern neural/embedding-based and state-of-the-art sentence-similarity baselines (e.g., BERT/RoBERTa-style encoders), which are highly relevant for paraphrase/entailment tasks, so important comparisons are missing. Related work coverage is described as thorough, but the paper states no limitations and the summary does not indicate acknowledgement when prior methods perform comparably, leaving the comparative analysis incomplete.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates TextFlow against a broad set of classical string and set-based baselines (Cosine, Euclidean, Jaccard, Levenshtein, LCS, etc.) on standard datasets (SNLI, RTE 1–3, SemEval) with reported hyperparameters, suggesting largely fair experimental conditions. However, the summary does not indicate comparison to recent neural sentence-embedding or transformer-based baselines (e.g., SBERT/BERT-style methods) and does not report acknowledging when prior methods perform comparably or better. While related work coverage is described as thorough, the empirical evaluation omits key modern baselines, so the comparisons are incomplete.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates TextFlow against a wide set of classical string- and set-based baselines (Cosine, Euclidean, Word Overlap, Dice, Jaccard, Damerau, Jaro-Winkler, Levenshtein, LCS) on multiple datasets (SNLI, RTE 1–3, SemEval subsets) and reports hyperparameters, indicating generally fair experimental conditions. However, it omits modern neural/embedding-based and state-of-the-art sentence-similarity baselines (e.g., BERT/RoBERTa-style encoders), which are highly relevant for paraphrase/entailment tasks, so important comparisons are missing. Related work coverage is described as thorough, but the paper states no limitations and the summary does not indicate acknowledgement when prior methods perform comparably, leaving the comparative analysis incomplete.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Based on the summary, the paper appears well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contribution—TextFlow and its DNA-alignment-inspired representation—early and clearly. Notation is consistent enough to report parameters (α, β, γ) and the optional XF layer in the neural variant, and experimental coverage is thorough (multiple datasets and standard baselines). Writing quality is described as adequate and related work coverage is thorough, but minor presentation gaps remain (no limitations are stated, code availability is not mentioned and the reported metric “CORE” is unclear), hence a small deduction. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized and presents its main contributions early (the TextFlow measure and its DNA-alignment inspiration are stated). Notation and methodological details seem adequately defined (parameters α, β, γ, default values, and an optional XF layer are described) and hyperparameters are reported; related work coverage is thorough and overall writing quality is described as adequate. However, the summary does not mention figures/tables/examples or discussion of limitations and code availability, indicating minor presentation gaps that keep this from being exceptional.

reviewer_a.justification: Based on the summary, the paper appears well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contribution—TextFlow and its DNA-alignment-inspired representation—early and clearly. Notation is consistent enough to report parameters (α, β, γ) and the optional XF layer in the neural variant, and experimental coverage is thorough (multiple datasets and standard baselines). Writing quality is described as adequate and related work coverage is thorough, but minor presentation gaps remain (no limitations are stated, code availability is not mentioned and the reported metric “CORE” is unclear), hence a small deduction.

main_claims: Proposes a novel text similarity measure called TextFlow, inspired by DNA sequence alignment algorithms.
Represents input text pairs as continuous curves and uses both word positions and sequence matching to compute similarity.
Reports encouraging experimental results on eight datasets for tasks including paraphrase detection, textual entailment recognition, and ranking relevance.
_summary.methodology: TextFlow represents pairs of texts as continuous curves and computes similarity by combining actual word positions with sequence matching, inspired by DNA alignment. The method includes parameters (α, β, γ) with default canonical values of 1 and an optional neural network with a dedicated XF layer (identity activation) to train these parameters on task-specific data.
